text.title: Thumbs Down → Eval — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that turn user thumbs-down into versioned eval-set rows without exploding CI cost or leaking PII.

rule.statement: The pipeline has EXACTLY 4 stages, in order: INGEST (normalize) → SCRUB (PII removal) → JUDGE (3 LLM judges vote) → ADMIT (PR to eval suite). Stages must run sequentially; skipping SCRUB to "save tokens" is forbidden.

rationale.source: Anthropic / OpenAI eval workflows + Presidio docs

rule.rationale: Out-of-order or partial pipelines either leak PII to evals (legal) or admit noise (poisoned eval set). Sequential structure is the only auditable shape.

rule.statement: SCRUB stage MUST detect and redact: email, phone, SSN/national-ID, names tagged PER, credit card, IP address, IBAN. Any candidate that fails scrub (entity remained after redaction) is REJECTED — never admitted to eval suite.

rationale.source: GDPR Art. 5 + CCPA / Presidio default categories

rule.rationale: Eval rows are persisted in code repos and run by future engineers. PII in evals propagates indefinitely. The reject-on-fail rule prevents the leak class entirely.

rule.statement: JUDGE stage uses 3 LLM judges (different prompts, optionally different models) scoring the candidate on (a) was the original response wrong, (b) is the prompt representative of real usage, (c) is this row likely to be reproducible. Admit threshold: ≥ 2 of 3 judges score "admit" on ALL three criteria.

rationale.source: Self-consistency literature (Wang et al. 2023) + LLM-as-judge research (Zheng et al. 2024)

rule.rationale: Single-judge admission is noisy (~70% agreement with human raters). 3-judge majority lifts agreement to > 85%. Different prompts/models reduce shared blind spots.

rule.statement: ADMIT stage caps growth at 50 new rows/week. Excess candidates are queued for the next week or dropped if older than 14 days. CI runtime stays bounded; eval set drift is paced.

rationale.source: Hamel Husain / Eugene Yan eval-ops essays

rule.rationale: Unbounded eval growth makes CI prohibitively slow and the team starts skipping eval runs. The cap is an operational constraint that keeps the eval suite usable.

rule.statement: STOP-THE-BLEED rule: if 3+ thumbs-down within a 60-min window cluster into the same failure pattern (cosine similarity > 0.85 on embedding), an alert pages on-call within 4 BUSINESS hours. This is independent of the eval pipeline and may NOT be muted by admit-stage backpressure.

rationale.source: SRE incident-response playbook + LLM-ops cohort failures

rule.rationale: An eval-set row added next week is irrelevant if the model is hallucinating right now. Stop-the-bleed is a parallel fast path for production incidents.

example.title: Single thumbs-down → admitted eval row
example.note: End-to-end, ~3 LLM calls + PR. Cap enforced. PII contained.

example.title: Skip SCRUB to save tokens (r2 violation)
example.code:
INGEST → JUDGE (skipping SCRUB) JUDGE admits row containing "John Smith, ssn 123-45-6789" ADMIT PR lands in evals/regressions/ repository.
3 months later: dev team runs evals on shared machine. Eval prompts logged to telemetry. SSN now in 6 systems.
example.note: r1 + r2 violation. SCRUB is non-skippable.
